title: 100 Mile House TSA Public Discussion Paper
summary: Title of the public discussion paper for the 100 Mile House Timber Supply Area.
source_quote: 100 Mile House TSA Public Discussion Paper

title: Introduction
summary: The introduction outlines the purpose of the timber supply review for the 100 Mile House TSA.
source_quote: Introduction

title: Introduction Summary
summary: This is the fourth timber supply review for the 100 Mile House TSA. The BC Ministry of Forests regularly reviews TSAs to determine new Allowable Annual Cuts (AAC) based on current management practices, economy, environment, and social conditions. The review aims to examine forest management practices, set a new AAC, and identify areas for improvement in future reviews. The paper summarizes the analysis results, with detailed data available in a separate package.
source_quote: The British Columbia Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations regularly reviews the timber supply... for all timber supply areas... This review, the fourth for the 100 Mile House TSA... Based on this review the chief forester will determine a new allowable annual cut (AAC)... The objectives of the timber supply review are to: examine relevant forest management practices... set a new AAC; and identify information to be improved... This public discussion paper provides a summary of the results...

title: Forest Act Reference
summary: The Chief Forester is mandated by Section 8 of the Forest Act to regularly review and set AACs for all 38 TSAs and 34 TFLs in BC.
source_quote: According to Section 8 of the Forest Act the chief forester must regularly review and set new AACs for all 38 TSAs and 34 TFLs in the Province of British Columbia (BC).

title: Timber supply
summary: The amount of timber forecast to be available for harvesting over a specified time period under a particular management regime.
source_quote: a Timber supply The amount of timber that is forecast to be available for harvesting over a specified time period, under a particular management regime.

title: Timber supply areas (TSAs)
summary: An integrated resource management unit established in accordance with Section 7 of the Forest Act.
source_quote: b Timber supply areas (TSAs) An integrated resource management unit established in accordance with Section 7 of the Forest Act.

title: Tree farm licences (TFLs)
summary: Provides rights to harvest timber and outlines responsibilities for forest management in a particular area.
source_quote: c Tree farm licences (TFLs) Provides rights to harvest timber and outlines responsibilities for forest management in a particular area.

title: Allowable annual cut (AAC)
summary: The maximum rate of timber harvest permitted each year from a specified area of land, usually expressed as cubic metres of wood per year.
source_quote: d Allowable annual cut (AAC) The maximum rate of timber harvest permitted each year from a specified area of land, usually expressed as cubic metres of wood per year.

section_path: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA
summary: This section details the historical context of AACs in the 100 Mile House TSA, including the impact of the mountain pine beetle epidemic.
source_quote: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA

section_path: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Timber supply review context
summary: Prior to the 2006 mountain pine beetle epidemic expansion, the AAC was 1.344 million cubic metres. In 2006, following an urgent review, the AAC was increased by 666,000 cubic metres (50%) to 2.0 million cubic metres. The current review aims to provide an overview of the process and analysis results, including harvest forecasts. The Chief Forester will determine the final AAC based on the analysis and other inputs, which may differ from the projections.
source_quote: Prior to the rapid expansion of the mountain pine beetle (MPB) epidemic (2006), the allowable annual cut (AAC) for the 100 Mile House TSA was 1.344 million cubic metres. In 2006... the AAC was increased by 666 000 cubic metres (50 percent) to the current level of 2.0 million cubic metres... This public discussion paper is being released to provide an overview of the timber supply review process... The AAC determined by the chief forester... may differ from the harvest projections...

section_path: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA
title: Pre-2006 AAC
summary: The allowable annual cut was 1.344 million cubic metres prior to the 2006 increase.
source_quote: the allowable annual cut (AAC) for the 100 Mile House TSA was 1.344 million cubic metres

section_path: Timber supply review in the 100 Mile House TSA
title: 2006 AAC Increase
summary: The AAC was increased by 666,000 cubic metres (50%) in 2006.
source_quote: the AAC was increased by 666 000 cubic metres (50 percent) to the current level of 2.0 million cubic metres

title: Socio-economic information
summary: Introduction to the socio-economic context of the TSA, including communities and employment statistics.
source_quote: Socio-economic information

title: Socio-economic information Summary
summary: The section outlines the main communities within the TSA, with 100 Mile House having a population of 1886 in 2011. It details employment sectors, noting the forest sector accounts for 26% of basic employment. Employment increased from 1488 to 1757 person-years post-2006 due to increased harvesting, though partially offset by efficiency gains. It lists mills using timber from the area.
source_quote: The main communities within the TSA are 100 Mile House... Based on the average harvest from 2007 to 2011, employment increased to 1757 person-years... Mills using timber from 100 Mile House TSA include...

title: Population of 100 Mile House
summary: Population count from 2011 Census.
source_quote: According to the 2011 Census, the population of 100 Mile House Municipality was 1886 persons.

title: Forest Sector Employment Share
summary: Percentage of total basic employment attributed to the forest sector.
source_quote: in the 100 Mile House District, the forest sector accounts for 26 percent of total basic employment.

title: First Nations
summary: Introduction to First Nations with asserted territories or interests in the TSA.
source_quote: First Nations

title: First Nations Summary
summary: Four First Nations have asserted territories in the core area: Tsq’escen, Xatl’tem/Stwecem’c, Bonaparte Indian Band, and High Bar First Nation. Many other First Nations have overlapping aboriginal interests. The Ministry intends to fulfill legal obligations to consult with First Nations.
source_quote: Four First Nations... have asserted territories in the core area... The Ministry of Forests... intends to continue to fulfill its legal obligations to consult with First Nations

title: Land use plans
summary: Introduction to land use regulations and plans affecting the TSA.
source_quote: Land use plans

title: Land use plans Summary
summary: The TSA is subject to the Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan (CCLUP). Forest development must align with land use objectives. Ungulate winter ranges and wildlife habitat areas for caribou and species at risk are established under the Government Actions Regulation.
source_quote: The 100 Mile House TSA lies within the area subject to the Cariboo-Chilcotin Land Use Plan... Ungulate winter ranges... have been established within the TSA in accordance with the Government Actions Regulation

title: Mountain pine beetle
summary: Introduction to the Mountain Pine Beetle infestation status and modeling.
source_quote: Mountain pine beetle

title: Mountain pine beetle Summary
summary: The BCMPB model projects pine mortality. Infestation peaked in 2006, with forecasts indicating 47 million cubic metres (81% of mature pine) would be killed. Current data shows the infestation has collapsed, with total actual killed volume at 42 million cubic metres (72% of mature pine).
source_quote: The MPB infestation within 100 Mile House TSA peaked in 2006... The total actual MPB-killed pine volume is about 42 million cubic metres or about 72 percent of the mature pine volume

title: Figure 3. Age class distribution
summary: Chart showing age class distribution of stands in the Crown forested land base.
source_quote: Figure 3. Age class distribution of stands in the Crown forested land base.

title: Figure 4. Cumulative volume of MPB-killed pine
summary: Chart showing current estimates of observed and projected cumulative volume of MPB-killed pine.
source_quote: Figure 4. Current estimate of observed and projected cumulative volume of MPB-killed pine in the THLB.

title: Timber supply analysis
summary: Introduction to the timber supply analysis and its role in AAC determination.
source_quote: Timber supply analysis

title: Summary
source_quote: Summary

summary: The base case harvest forecast indicates the current AAC of 2.0 million cubic metres can be maintained for seven years before declining. Sensitivity analysis suggests prioritizing salvage of beetle-killed stands and limiting live volume harvest to minimize decline. The provincial chief forester's AAC determination is a professional judgment under Section 8 of the Forest Act and is not limited to timber supply analysis results.
source_quote: The base case harvest forecast indicates that the current AAC of 2.0 million cubic metres can be maintained for seven years before declining to the mid-term harvest level. Therefore, the current AAC cannot be sustained for the full 10 years of the determination period. Sensitivity analysis results show that a continued priority on the salvage of beetle-killed stands and a limit on the harvest of live volume are necessary to minimize the decline in timber supply when salvage is complete. The provincial chief forester’s AAC determination is a judgement based on his professional experience and his consideration of a wide range of information as required under Section 8 of the Forest Act. An AAC is neither the result of a calculation nor limited to the results of timber supply analysis; therefore, the new AAC may not be the same as any of the harvest forecasts, including the base case, described in this PDP.

summary: Sensitivity analysis shows that continued priority on salvage of beetle-killed stands and limiting live volume harvest minimizes timber supply decline after salvage is complete.
source_quote: Sensitivity analysis results show that a continued priority on the salvage of beetle-killed stands and a limit on the harvest of live volume are necessary to minimize the decline in timber supply when salvage is complete.

summary: Current Allowable Annual Cut (AAC) is 2.0 million cubic metres.
source_quote: the current AAC of 2.0 million cubic metres

summary: The current AAC can be maintained for seven years.
source_quote: maintained for seven years

summary: The determination period is 10 years.
source_quote: full 10 years of the determination period

summary: Section 8 of the Forest Act requires consideration of a wide range of information for AAC determination.
source_quote: required under Section 8 of the Forest Act

title: Your input is needed
source_quote: Your input is needed

summary: Public input is requested for the Allowable Annual Cut determination. Comments must be sent to the District Manager by March 9, 2013. Responses may be subject to the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act. Contact information for David McArthur and the Forest Analysis Branch is provided.
source_quote: Public input is a vital part of establishing the allowable annual cut. Feedback is welcomed on any aspect of this public discussion paper or any other issues related to the timber supply review for the 100 Mile House TSA. Ministry staff would be pleased to answer questions to help you prepare your response. Please send your comments to the district manager at the address below. Your comments will be accepted until March 9, 2013. You may identify yourself on the response if you wish. If you do, you are reminded that responses will be subject to the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act and may be made public. If the responses are made public, personal identifiers will be removed before the responses are released. For more information or to send your comments, contact: District Manager 100 Mile House District Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations 300 S Cariboo Highway 100 Mile House, British Columbia V0K 2E0 Or call: David McArthur, Stewardship Forester 100 Mile House District Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations Phone: (250) 395-7862 Electronic mail: David.McArthur@gov.bc.ca Further information regarding the technical details of the timber supply analysis are available on request by contacting Forests.ForestAnalysisBranchOffice@gov.bc.ca Visit the Forest Analysis and Inventory Branch web site at http://www.for.gov.bc.ca/hts/

summary: Comments must be received by March 9, 2013.
source_quote: Your comments will be accepted until March 9, 2013.

summary: Responses are subject to the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act.
source_quote: responses will be subject to the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act
